review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy an automated alert workflow in your CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that triggers SMS/WhatsApp notifications via Twilio within 15 minutes of any flight‑time change, including the new departure time and a link to rebook options. | - Integrate FlightAware’s API with your passenger portal so that any schedule adjustment automatically updates all affected itineraries and displays a “7‑hour shift” banner with a one‑click “Reschedule now” button. | - Launch a self‑service compensation engine powered by Zendesk Guide that issues a travel voucher or mileage credit automatically when the system detects a >6‑hour time move, reducing manual processing time from days to minutes. | - Schedule a weekly Power BI dashboard review meeting where operations managers flag any schedule changes exceeding 5 hours and commit to corrective actions within 24 hours, tracking compliance metrics such as “% of on‑time adjustments” and “average resolution time.”
rationale: All recommendations directly address the 7-hour flight change issue with precise tools (Twilio, FlightAware, Zendesk Guide, Power BI) and measurable actions. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete metrics. Feasibility is reduced to 3 because integrating FlightAware API and building a self-service compensation engine require moderate effort, cross-team coordination, and likely new development—beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

issue: Customer service representatives gave contradictory information and seemed incompetent.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time knowledge‑base sync (e.g., using Zendesk Guide) so every agent’s desktop automatically pulls the latest policy versions before answering any call or chat, eliminating conflicting scripts. | - Institute a weekly “Truth‑Check” audit where two randomly selected calls are reviewed in a shared Google Sheet; if answers differ, the responsible reps receive a corrective‑action ticket in Jira with a 48‑hour resolution deadline. | - Implement a dual‑agent handoff protocol in Salesforce Service Cloud: when an interaction is escalated, the second agent must verify the first agent’s notes and sign off digitally before closing the case. | - Schedule bi‑weekly 30‑minute role‑play drills on the Gong.io conversation analytics platform, focusing on handling common contradictions and measuring improvement via a post‑session competency scorecard.
rationale: All recommendations directly address contradictory service by targeting knowledge inconsistency, verification, and training. Each specifies real tools (Zendesk Guide, Jira, Salesforce Service Cloud, Gong.io) and measurable actions. Actionability is high with clear steps. Feasibility is moderate (3) due to required integration of Gong.io for analytics and dual-agent sign-off in Salesforce, which may require configuration and training, but no major overhaul. Not trivial (5), but not high-effort (2).

issue: Refund processing took excessively long, with repeated 6‑week delays.
fixes: Deploy a refund SLA dashboard in Zendesk (or ServiceNow) that triggers automatic escalation emails after 48 hours if a case remains “Pending” and assigns a dedicated “Refunds” queue with a 7‑day first‑response target. | - Integrate Stripe’s Refund API with your order management system so refunds are initiated instantly upon approval, eliminating manual batch processing that causes delays. | - Build a weekly KPI report in Power BI that tracks average refund cycle time, number of repeat‑request cases, and flags any ticket exceeding 10 business days for immediate review by the Finance Operations Lead. | - Create a self‑service refund portal powered by Formstack that captures all required documentation upfront; set up automated validation rules to reject incomplete submissions and reduce manual handoffs by at least 20 %. | - Conduct a monthly audit using SQL queries against the ERP (e.g., SAP) to identify high‑volume refund categories and route them through a dedicated “Express Refund” workflow in Microsoft Flow, guaranteeing a maximum 3‑business‑day resolution.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the 6-week refund delay issue with precise tools and measurable actions. Each is actionable and concrete, naming specific systems (Zendesk, Stripe, Power BI, Formstack, SAP, Microsoft Flow, SQL). Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to requiring cross-system integrations (e.g., Stripe API with order management, ERP audits via SQL) and workflow changes that are not trivial, though not impossible. No points deducted for format or clarity.

review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy self‑service check‑in kiosks (e.g., NCR SelfServ) at every entrance and integrate them with the property’s PMS via API so guests can complete registration and receive room keys within 5 minutes of arrival. | - Use a workforce‑management platform such as Kronos Workforce Central to run real‑time queue analytics and automatically add staff when average wait time exceeds 10 minutes, ensuring agent coverage matches demand spikes. | - Implement a digital queue board (e.g., QLess) that pushes SMS updates to guests, allowing them to join the virtual line remotely and reducing physical congestion by up to 40 %. | - Conduct weekly 15‑minute “speed‑audit” drills where agents record each step in a standardized checklist on GoCanvas mobile forms; track completion times and tie results to performance bonuses.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root cause of long check-in wait times and inefficient agents. Each is highly actionable with clear tools and steps. Concrete tools (NCR, Kronos, QLess, GoCanvas) and measurable outcomes (5-minute check-in, 10-minute threshold, 40% congestion reduction) ensure concreteness. Feasibility is 3 because while tools exist, integrating PMS APIs, deploying kiosks, and rolling out SMS queue systems require moderate coordination, budget, and time—beyond 'trivial' or 'low effort'.

issue: In-flight service was slow and unsatisfactory despite airline's reputation.
fixes: Deploy a mobile “Service Request” app (e.g., FlightOps) integrated with crew tablets so passengers can instantly signal seat‑back issues, and set a KPI that 80 % of requests are acknowledged within 90 seconds of submission. | - Conduct daily 15‑minute “micro‑huddle” debriefs on the flight deck using a shared Google Sheet that records any delay incidents; tie the resulting data to crew performance bonuses based on a target ≤5‑minute average response time per cabin zone. | - Install QR‑code feedback stations at each galley door linked to SurveyMonkey; automatically trigger a post‑flight email with a 10‑question NPS survey and require a minimum 85 % response rate before the next route’s service plan is finalized. | - Implement a predictive staffing model in Power BI that forecasts passenger load by meal timing and assigns an extra cabin attendant when projected demand exceeds a threshold of 150 % of normal service capacity, ensuring consistent pace regardless of load factor.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to in-flight service delays and explicitly name concrete tools (FlightOps, Google Sheets, SurveyMonkey, Power BI). Each is actionable with clear steps and measurable outcomes. However, feasibility is severely compromised: deploying a new mobile app (FlightOps), requiring crew tablet integration, and implementing predictive staffing via Power BI with real-time thresholds demands significant investment, cross-system integration, and training—far beyond trivial or low effort. These are high-effort, high-cost changes, justifying a 2 on feasibility.

issue: Cabin crew repeatedly bumped into the passenger's seat without apologizing.
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑crew interaction checklist in the existing mobile SOP app (e.g., SITA CabinOps) that requires staff to log a brief apology and “no contact” confirmation after any seat access; compliance is tracked daily via the system’s analytics dashboard. | - Schedule quarterly VR scenario‑based drills using Strivr’s “Airline Safety & Customer Interaction” module, measuring performance by average “apology response time” recorded on the airline’s LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors). | - Install discreet pressure‑sensor mats at aisle seats linked to the crew’s handheld devices (e.g., Panasonic TOUGHBOOK); when a bump is detected, the device automatically displays a scripted apology prompt and logs the event for weekly KPI reporting. | - Launch a micro‑learning series on Axonify that delivers a 2‑minute video on respectful seat access followed by a quiz with a ≥90 % pass threshold, and require completion within two weeks for all cabin personnel, with completion rates monitored in the Axonify dashboard.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (SITA CabinOps, Strivr, SAP SuccessFactors, Axonify, Panasonic TOUGHBOOK) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to the high cost and complexity of installing pressure-sensor mats on aisle seats, which requires hardware deployment, integration with handheld devices, and event logging infrastructure—making it a high-effort, expensive change not trivial to implement.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a digital pre‑flight inspection checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that requires cabin crew to photograph and sign off any seat‑related defect before loading, automatically logging findings for audit. | - Install QR‑code stickers on all seat tables linking to a 15‑second video demonstrating proper installation; crew scan the code during the “seat readiness” step and confirm via the airline’s mobile SOP app. | - Introduce a weekly “Seat Integrity Drill” where a random sample of seats is dismantled and reassembled using the manufacturer’s approved tools, with results recorded in a Power BI dashboard targeting <0.5% defective units per month. | - Equip maintenance trucks with portable vibration sensors that trigger an alert if a seat’s table bracket exceeds the specified tolerance, prompting immediate corrective action before boarding. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey sent through the airline’s loyalty app asking passengers to rate “Seat condition disclosure” on a 1‑5 scale; aggregate scores feed into a monthly KPI report reviewed by operations leadership.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (iAuditor, Power BI, mobile SOP app, vibration sensors) and measurable outcomes. However, feasibility is severely compromised: deploying vibration sensors on maintenance trucks, requiring new hardware and integration, is high effort and costly—especially without evidence of prior investment. The weekly drill also demands significant labor and coordination. These exceed low-effort thresholds, warranting a 2/5 despite strong alignment elsewhere.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy digital signage (e.g., Samsung Smart Signage) at the immigration hall and baggage claim that displays real‑time “Boarding Pass Collection Point – Gate X” updates pulled from the airline’s Amadeus PSS, automatically refreshed every minute. | - Integrate a push notification trigger into the airline’s mobile app (using Firebase Cloud Messaging) that sends a personalized “Your boarding pass is ready at Counter Y” message once the passenger’s itinerary is checked‑in via the app. | - Add a QR code printed on all boarding passes that links to a short video hosted on Vimeo (or YouTube) showing a step‑by‑step walk‑through of the collection point location; scan the code with any smartphone camera to instantly view the video. | - Schedule a daily 10‑minute briefing for ground staff using Microsoft Teams where the operations manager reviews the day’s flight schedules and confirms which counters will host boarding‑pass issuance, then logs the assignment in a shared Google Sheet for instant access by front‑line agents.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue: lack of clear boarding pass collection information in Singapore. Each is specific, actionable, and names real tools (Amadeus PSS, Firebase, Vimeo, Google Sheets, Microsoft Teams). However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to reliance on multiple new integrations (Amadeus-to-digital-signage sync, app push triggers, QR video hosting), which require coordination, development effort, and potential vendor contracts—moderate to high effort, not trivial or low-cost.

As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: In-flight crew served meals while passengers were dozing and expected tables to be set, leading to poor service timing and inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy a “Sleep‑Mode” indicator in the airline’s cabin management software (e.g., SITA Cabin) that automatically hides tray‑cart announcements when seat‑belt signs are on; crew receive real‑time push notifications via their handheld devices to delay meal service until the sign is off. | - Integrate a pre‑flight service checklist into the crew’s mobile app (using CrewConnect) that requires a mandatory “Table Ready” confirmation before any meal cart can be unlocked, with timestamps logged for audit. | - Install overhead cabin sensors linked to the crew’s tablet dashboard (via IBM Watson IoT) that detect passenger movement patterns; the system flags low‑activity periods (e.g., >10 min after meal announcement) and prompts crew to pause service until activity resumes. | - Schedule a weekly 15‑minute debrief using a Power BI service‑timing report that compares actual meal‑service start times versus passenger wake‑up data from seat‑back sensors, targeting a <2‑minute deviation metric and adjusting crew shift assignments accordingly.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (SITA Cabin, CrewConnect, IBM Watson IoT, Power BI) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: installing overhead sensors, integrating IoT systems, and relying on seat-back sensor data are not trivial, require significant capital investment, and involve cross-functional engineering and privacy considerations—far beyond 'existing tools' or 'today' rollout.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: The quality of premium economy meals was no better than economy fare, disappointing given the higher cost.
fixes: Deploy a digital menu‑engineering platform (e.g., MenuCalc) to benchmark premium‑economy dishes against economy standards; adjust recipes until the premium‑economy score exceeds the economy baseline by at least 10 % on flavor, portion size, and ingredient freshness metrics. | - Introduce a quarterly “Premium‑Economy Meal Audit” using iAuditor checklists that rate presentation, temperature, and waste per service; trigger corrective actions when any rating falls below 85 %. | - Source premium ingredients through a vetted supplier portal (e.g., SAP Ariba) with a minimum 20 % premium over economy pricing and enforce compliance via monthly spend analytics reports. | - Launch a post‑flight QR‑code survey linked to Qualtrics that asks passengers to rate meal quality on a 1‑5 scale; automatically flag any premium‑economy rating ≤3 for immediate recipe review and kitchen coaching. | - Implement Kitchen Display System (KDS) alerts in the galley that lock premium‑economy meal preparation times to ≤15 minutes, ensuring consistent cooking temperatures and reducing service delays that impact food quality.
rationale: All recommendations are directly relevant to food quality in premium economy, specify exact tools (MenuCalc, iAuditor, SAP Ariba, Qualtrics, KDS), and are actionable with clear steps. However, feasibility is low: deploying a new KDS alert system, integrating multiple third-party platforms, and enforcing supplier compliance via spend analytics require significant IT integration, cross-functional coordination, and budget—making this high-effort (score 2), not trivial or low-cost.

review_text: The ground crew were unreasonable and very rude. We were denied boarding as according to them we were 4 minutes late.  The ground crew did a lousy job of offloading our luggage. We had to wait almost half a day in the baggage carousel before filling a missing bag report.
issue: Ground crew were rude and denied boarding for a minor 4‑minute delay
fixes: Deploy a mobile “Delay‑Response” checklist in the crew’s handheld device (e.g., iPad with ServiceNow Field Service) that triggers automatically when a flight is delayed > 5 minutes; crew must confirm the reason before denying boarding and the timestamp logs to compliance dashboards. | - Integrate a real‑time passenger‑rights module into the airline’s CRM (Salesforce Service Cloud) that sends pre‑approved compensation vouchers (travel credit, lounge access) within 15 minutes of any denied‑boarding incident, reducing perceived rudeness while documenting resolution times. | - Launch quarterly 45‑minute VR empathy simulations on the Strivr platform where ground staff experience a simulated minor delay scenario, followed by a post‑session quiz scored against a ≥90 % pass threshold to certify competency. | - Install QR‑code feedback kiosks at gate areas linked to Medallia Mobile surveys that capture instant ratings (1‑10) for crew courtesy during delays; set a KPI to keep average rating ≥ 8.0 and trigger corrective coaching alerts for scores below 7.0.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to service quality and the specific issue of rude behavior during delays. Each is actionable with clear steps and uses concrete tools (ServiceNow, Salesforce, Strivr, Medallia). However, feasibility is low: VR simulations (Strivr), real-time CRM integration, and QR kiosks with automated alerts require significant investment, cross-team coordination, and new infrastructure—none of which are trivial or low-cost. This disqualifies a 5 on feasibility; 2 is justified given the scale and cost of deployment.

issue: Luggage was mishandled, leading to a half‑day wait and a missing bag report
fixes: Deploy RFID tags on all checked bags and integrate them with the airline’s baggage management system (e.g., SITA BagJourney) so any deviation triggers an automatic alert within 5 minutes. | - Create a “Mishandled Luggage Response” SOP that mandates a maximum 15‑minute investigation window, assigns a dedicated Baggage Supervisor via a shift‑roster in Deputy, and logs every case in ServiceNow for real‑time KPI tracking. | - Launch a mobile self‑service portal powered by Amadeus SkySuite that lets passengers scan their boarding pass QR code to file a missing‑bag report instantly, automatically creating a priority ticket in the service desk queue. | - Implement predictive maintenance on conveyor belts using Siemens MindSphere analytics; set a threshold that alerts maintenance crews when belt speed or alignment deviates >2 % from baseline, reducing mechanical failures that cause luggage jams.
rationale: All recommendations directly address baggage mishandling root causes with specific tools (SITA, Deputy, ServiceNow, Amadeus, Siemens MindSphere). Each is actionable and concrete, naming real systems and measurable thresholds. Feasibility is rated 3 due to moderate effort: RFID deployment, system integrations, and new portal development require cross-team coordination, budget, and time—beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

review_text: Our first flight was delayed more than one hour, so we lost our connecting flight. The new booking provided was horrible. I was travelling with my four-year-old and the new seat provided is far away, and we were separated. All the family members are separated for the whole journey. Miscommunication between the crew members made us to walk between the terminals for 2 times. Didn't get any help from the crew, even though we requested to provide a seat together.
issue: Flight was delayed over an hour, causing a missed connecting flight
fixes: Deploy a real‑time disruption dashboard (e.g., Amadeus AirportConnect) that automatically flags flights exceeding a 15‑minute delay threshold and triggers an instant rebooking workflow in Sabre Red Workspace, offering passengers alternative connections within 10 minutes of detection. | - Integrate Twilio SMS/WhatsApp alerts with the airline’s operations center so any delay >30 minutes sends passengers personalized rebooking options and live status updates, reducing manual outreach time by at least 40 %. | - Implement predictive analytics using IBM Watson Studio on historical flight‑delay data to identify high‑risk departure pairs and schedule buffer slots of up to 20 minutes for those routes, cutting missed‑connection incidents by 25 % within six months. | - Establish a dedicated “Disruption Response Team” staffed with two agents per shift, equipped with a Trello board synced to the scheduling system; every new delay creates a card that assigns a mitigation task (rebook, provide ground transport voucher, etc.) with a 5‑minute SLA for completion.
rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address flight delays and missed connections. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but assume access to specific tools without confirming availability. Concreteness is 5: all tools (Amadeus, Sabre, Twilio, IBM Watson, Trello) are named explicitly. Feasibility is 2: implementing IBM Watson Studio, real-time dashboards, and a dedicated team with SLAs requires significant budget, integration effort, and cross-functional coordination—far beyond trivial or low effort.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy an automated “Missed‑Connection Alert” in the airline’s operations center (e.g., using IBM Watson Orchestrate) that triggers when a scheduled arrival exceeds the buffer time set for each connecting gate, instantly routing passengers to a standby lounge and securing alternative seats on the next available flight. | - Integrate real‑time delay forecasts from FlightAware into the crew scheduling software (e.g., Sabre AirVision) so that ground staff receive at least 15 minutes’ advance notice of potential landing delays, enabling proactive rebooking before boarding begins. | - Implement a dedicated “Rapid Rebooking Hub” staffed by two agents per shift, equipped with a Power BI dashboard showing live connection windows; each hub processes missed‑connection cases within 10 minutes of detection, guaranteeing a confirmed new itinerary. | - Launch a post‑incident compensation workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that automatically issues a $100 travel voucher plus a personalized apology email via SendGrid within 24 hours of the disruption, tracking redemption rates for continuous improvement.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root cause of missed connections due to landing delays. Each specifies tools (IBM Watson Orchestrate, Sabre AirVision, Power BI, Salesforce, SendGrid), measurable actions (15-minute notice, 10-minute processing, $100 voucher), and clear outcomes. Feasibility is scored 3 because while tools are real, integrating FlightAware with Sabre AirVision and deploying a new Rapid Rebooking Hub requires cross-department coordination and likely budget—moderate effort, not trivial.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a ticketing platform (e.g., Zendesk) integrated with your website’s “Contact Us” form and set an SLA that any new ticket must receive a first‑response within 15 minutes, with automatic escalation if the response timer exceeds 30 minutes. | - Install a cloud‑based call‑center analytics solution such as Talkdesk to monitor average answer time (AAE) and queue length in real time, triggering alerts when AAE > 45 seconds so supervisors can reallocate agents instantly. | - Create a dedicated “Live Chat & Email Response” team with a rotating shift schedule and track their performance by measuring first‑contact resolution rate on a daily dashboard in Power BI. | - Publish a self‑service knowledge base using Confluence linked to the contact forms, and embed a “Solution Found” button that automatically closes tickets after users click it, reducing inbound requests by at least 10 % within six months.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the customer's issue of unresponsiveness via multiple channels. Each is highly actionable with clear steps and specific tools (Zendesk, Talkdesk, Power BI, Confluence). Concreteness is strong with named systems and measurable outcomes. Feasibility is rated 3 because while tools are real, implementing a new dedicated team, real-time analytics, and automated ticket closure requires cross-functional coordination and integration effort beyond trivial rollout.

fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based catering management platform (e.g., CaterTech) integrated with the airline’s reservation system so passengers can pre‑order meals up to 24 hours before departure and receive real‑time status updates via the airline’s mobile app. | - Install IoT temperature sensors linked to a dashboard (such as Azure IoT Central) in all galley freezers and ovens; set alerts when temperatures deviate from optimal ranges, ensuring consistent meal quality throughout the flight. | - Partner with a regional gourmet caterer to deliver portion‑controlled, chef‑prepared dishes that are batch‑cooked and sealed at origin, then stored in insulated containers that maintain serving temperature for at least two hours after delivery. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey sent through the airline’s CRM (e.g., Salesforce Marketing Cloud) asking passengers to rate food quality within 30 minutes of landing; feed the results into a monthly KPI report and trigger corrective actions if average scores fall below 8/10.
rationale: The response is highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (CaterTech, Azure IoT Central, Salesforce Marketing Cloud) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is scored 2 due to high effort: deploying a new cloud catering platform, integrating with reservation systems, installing IoT sensors across all aircraft, partnering with a gourmet caterer, and launching a new survey system all require significant budget, cross-functional coordination, and time—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: No touchscreen TV or comparable in‑flight entertainment provided.
fixes: Deploy seat‑back tablets (e.g., Panasonic Avionics eX3) on all aircraft within 12 months, installing them via a phased retrofit schedule tracked in SAP PM work orders. | - Launch a cloud‑based streaming service (such as Viasat’s ViaSat Connect) integrated with the existing cabin management system, and complete rollout verification by running a 24‑hour pilot flight on three routes per month. | - Equip each seat with a USB‑C port and enable a “Personal Device Mode” in the CMS that streams content from passengers’ phones/tablets through the airline’s Content Distribution Network (CDN), tested on two aircraft before full fleet deployment. | - Implement an automated passenger feedback loop using SurveyMonkey linked to ServiceNow tickets, triggering a corrective action workflow when ≥5% of respondents rate “Entertainment Availability” ≤3 stars during any flight cycle.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Panasonic Avionics eX3, Viasat, SAP PM, SurveyMonkey, ServiceNow, CDN) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is severely compromised: retrofitting all aircraft with seat-back tablets in 12 months is a high-cost, high-effort project requiring major capital investment, not trivial or low-effort. The 24-hour pilot flight rollout and full fleet deployment of streaming services also imply significant infrastructure and coordination. Thus, while technically sound, these are not feasible within a budget-conscious COO’s constraints.

issue: Seat was average and aircraft was noisy, leading to uncomfortable travel experience.
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑wide acoustic insulation retrofit (e.g., Aerolite + Acoustic Barrier System) and schedule quarterly noise‑level audits with calibrated handheld decibel meters to reduce cabin sound pressure by at least 3 dB. | - Replace existing seats with ergonomic recliner models equipped with adjustable lumbar support and memory foam cushions; track seat‑comfort scores via the post‑flight Qualtrics survey trigger (target ≥ 85% “comfortable” rating within three months). | - Install IoT‑enabled vibration sensors on the fuselage and cabin floor, feeding real‑time data into a predictive maintenance dashboard in IBM Maximo; set a threshold alert for any vibration > 0.5 g so repairs can be scheduled before passenger discomfort occurs. | - Launch a “Quiet‑Cabin Zone” pilot program using the airline’s Amadeus Mobile app to allocate rows where passengers can book seats that receive extra sound‑absorbing padding and are serviced only during off‑peak flight times, measuring success by a 15% drop in cabin‑noise complaints per month.
rationale: While all recommendations are highly relevant, concrete, and actionable in detail, they are entirely infeasible. Retrofitting entire cabins with Aerolite, deploying IoT sensors across fuselages, launching a new app-based zone program, and requiring real-time dashboard integration with IBM Maximo are all financially ruinous and operationally impossible at scale. No existing airline could implement these in the short term without massive capital expenditure and structural overhaul, warranting a 1 on feasibility.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy a mobile boarding‑pass verification app (e.g., SITA Mobile) that scans QR codes at the gate and automatically routes eligible passengers to pre‑assigned zones, cutting manual sorting time by up to 30 %. | - Integrate an AI‑driven baggage‑sorting workflow in the cabin‑crew tablet (using Microsoft Power BI embedded dashboards) that flags oversized items and re‑routes them to dedicated carts before boarding begins. | - Implement a real‑time seat‑allocation engine (such as Amadeus Altéa’s “Dynamic Seat Management”) that groups passengers based on zone proximity and push notifications, reducing the average walk‑distance per passenger by approximately 15 %. | - Conduct a weekly 20‑minute “Gate‑Flow Drill” using the airline’s digital SOP platform (e.g., Lufthansa Systems FlightOps) where crew run through the new boarding sequence, capturing performance metrics and adjusting scripts until boarding time drops below the target threshold.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to boarding delays, with concrete tools (SITA Mobile, Power BI, Amadeus Altéa, FlightOps) and measurable outcomes. Actionability is strong—each step is clear and executable. Concreteness is maximal, naming real systems and KPIs. However, feasibility is rated 2: implementing AI-driven baggage workflows via Power BI dashboards on crew tablets, dynamic seat management, and full digital SOP drills across crews requires significant integration, training, and system overhaul—far beyond trivial or low effort. These are high-effort, high-cost changes, not feasible for immediate rollout.

issue: Loyalty status (Solitaire-PPS) was not recognized on board
fixes: Deploy a real‑time PPS verification script in the airline’s reservation system (e.g., Sabre/Amadeus) that cross‑checks Solitaire tier against the loyalty database via a REST API call before boarding; any mismatch automatically generates a “Status Pending” flag displayed on the crew tablet. | - Configure the cabin management system (e.g., SITA CabinConnect) to pull the latest Solitaire status from the central CRM (Salesforce Loyalty Cloud) every five minutes and push it to seat‑back screens and crew handhelds, ensuring instant recognition at boarding gates and onboard. | - Add a mandatory pre‑flight validation step in the ground‑crew SOP checklist using a mobile app such as iAuditor, requiring agents to scan the passenger’s loyalty card with a QR code scanner and confirm Solitaire tier before allowing them to board. | - Implement an automated post‑flight reconciliation job in the data warehouse that compares the actual tier served versus the recorded tier, flags any mismatches, and sends a corrective email to the relevant operations team within two hours of departure.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue of Solitaire-PPS status not being recognized at boarding. Each specifies real tools (Sabre/Amadeus, Salesforce Loyalty Cloud, iAuditor, SITA CabinConnect), exact actions, and measurable outcomes. The only deduction is in feasibility: while the recommendations are technically sound, implementing real-time API calls, automated reconciliation jobs, and system-wide SOP changes across multiple platforms requires cross-department coordination, integration effort, and likely a moderate budget—justifying a 3/5. No other flaws are present.

issue: Seat width was narrow in a 3‑3‑3 layout and recline was minimal, similar to budget airlines
fixes: Deploy a seat‑width validation test using the 3D CAD model (e.g., Siemens NX) and set a minimum target of 18.5 inches for economy seats; any design that falls below this threshold is rejected before production. | - Integrate a real‑time passenger comfort sensor (such as Recaro’s “ComfortSense” pressure mats) into the cabin management system to collect seat‑width and recline data after every flight, triggering alerts when average scores dip below 80 % of the desired level. | - Switch to a modular seat frame with interchangeable backrests (e.g., Zodiac Aerospace’s “FlexFit” system) and schedule quarterly audits to ensure at least two backrest options are installed on all aircraft, allowing passengers to select a higher‑recline setting without sacrificing legroom. | - Launch a post‑flight NPS survey question (“Rate your seat comfort on a scale of 1‑10”) via the airline’s mobile app, automatically routing scores ≤6 to a dedicated “Seat Improvement” task force within Jira for rapid remediation cycles. | - Negotiate a bulk purchase agreement with a certified ergonomic supplier (e.g., Collins Aerospace) that mandates a minimum seat‑pitch of 31‑32 inches and a maximum recline limit of 20 degrees for economy rows, with quarterly compliance reports reviewed by the cabin engineering team.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to seat comfort, explicitly name real tools/processes (e.g., Siemens NX, Jira, Recaro’s ComfortSense), and are actionably described. However, feasibility is severely compromised: bulk purchase agreements with suppliers, integrating new sensor systems, and replacing seat frames across fleets require massive capital investment, cross-departmental coordination, and months of engineering work—clearly exceeding 'low effort' or 'trivial' thresholds. This makes the entire proposal unfeasible in a budget-conscious context.

issue: Cabin crew ignored the passenger’s after‑dinner drink request, showing inattentiveness
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑crew mobile checklist app (e.g., ServiceNow Mobile) that forces crew members to acknowledge and log each passenger’s beverage request before they can move on to the next seat; the system automatically timestamps entries for audit. | - Install a “Request‑Confirmation” button on the in‑seat IFE screen that sends an instant alert to the nearest crew member via the airline’s CrewConnect messaging hub, ensuring a response within 30 seconds. | - Introduce a post‑flight micro‑survey linked to the passenger’s reservation (via Salesforce Marketing Cloud) asking “Did you receive your after‑meal beverage promptly?” with a target compliance score ≥ 95 % monitored weekly. | - Schedule a 15‑minute daily debrief using the airline’s video‑review portal (e.g., FlightOps Analytics) where supervisors review any logged “request‑ignored” incidents and coach crews on immediate corrective actions.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the specific issue of ignored after-dinner drink requests. Each is highly actionable, specifies real tools (ServiceNow Mobile, CrewConnect, Salesforce Marketing Cloud, FlightOps Analytics), and includes measurable KPIs. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to reliance on multiple new or specialized systems (e.g., in-seat IFE integration, video-review portal) that likely require cross-team coordination and budget, making rollout non-trivial despite existing tools being named.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: No soap was provided in the washroom
fixes: Deploy a digital audit checklist (e.g., iAuditor) with a “Soap Dispenser Full” field; staff complete it after every shift and the app automatically flags any non‑compliant entries for immediate corrective action. | - Install sensor‑activated soap dispensers integrated with the existing building management system (BMS) so low‑soap alerts trigger a restock ticket in ServiceNow within 15 minutes of depletion. | - Contract a local cleaning vendor to perform a weekly inventory count and place a reorder of soap when stock falls below a preset threshold, tracked via a Google Sheet dashboard updated nightly. | - Post QR codes on each sink linking to a one‑click request form in Microsoft Teams; any guest without soap can scan the code, submit a request, and receive real‑time confirmation that the dispenser is being replenished immediately.
rationale: All recommendations are relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (iAuditor, ServiceNow, Google Sheets, Microsoft Teams). However, feasibility is rated 2: installing sensor-activated dispensers integrated with BMS and requiring real-time alerts and ticket generation is high effort, not trivial. Contracting a vendor for weekly counts and using a Google Sheet dashboard adds operational overhead. These are not low-cost or immediate fixes, violating the strict feasibility threshold for a 5.

issue: Disinfectant kits were not offered as advertised unless specifically requested
fixes: Deploy a mandatory “Disinfection Offer” checkbox in the order‑entry workflow of your CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that triggers automatic inclusion of the kit whenever a request is made, preventing manual omissions. | - Install QR code stickers on checkout screens linking to a pre‑populated disinfectant‑kit add‑on page in Shopify’s “Order Summary,” ensuring customers see the option at every purchase point without staff intervention. | - Schedule a weekly compliance audit using Power Automate to extract all orders where the disinfectant checkbox was unchecked, flagging them for immediate manager review and corrective action. | - Introduce a KPI dashboard in Tableau that tracks “% of orders with disinfectant kits included vs. advertised” and set a target ≥ 95%, reviewing results in a monthly operations meeting.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue: kits not being offered unless requested. Each specifies concrete tools (Salesforce, Shopify, Power Automate, Tableau), measurable actions, and clear KPIs. Steps are actionable and unambiguous. However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to reliance on multiple integrated systems (Shopify, Salesforce, Power Automate, Tableau) requiring cross-system coordination, configuration, and likely dev resources—moderate effort, not trivial or low-cost.

issue: Mask‑wearing rules were not enforced by the crew, allowing passengers to wear masks improperly
fixes: Deploy handheld “Mask Compliance” tablets (e.g., iPad + iAuditor) at every gate and on board; crew scan QR codes on passenger tickets, verify mask fit and positioning in real time, and log any non‑compliance for immediate follow‑up. | - Integrate mask‑enforcement checkpoints into the existing Crew Performance Management dashboard (Power BI) with KPIs such as “% of inspections completed” and “% of violations remedied within 5 minutes,” triggering automated alerts when thresholds drop below 95 %. | - Schedule weekly 15‑minute VR scenario drills on the Strivr platform where cabin crews practice spot‑checking mask adherence while receiving instant feedback on compliance rates. | - Install AI‑enabled mask detection cameras (e.g., Verkada CCTV with Face Mask Detection SDK) at entry points that flag improper masks and push real‑time notifications to crew handheld devices, ensuring enforcement is logged automatically.
rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address mask enforcement gaps. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but require interpretation (e.g., 'integrate into dashboard' lacks exact configuration steps). Concreteness is 5: specific tools (iPad, iAuditor, Power BI, Strivr, Verkada) and KPIs named. Feasibility is 2: AI cameras and VR drills require high effort, significant budget, and coordination—impractical for immediate rollout. Cost and infrastructure barriers disqualify it from higher feasibility.

issue: Crew appeared overworked and demotivated, affecting overall service
fixes: Deploy a workforce‑management platform (e.g., Kronos WFM) integrated with real‑time labor analytics to forecast peak demand and automatically generate shift schedules that keep average weekly hours per employee below the industry benchmark of 40 hours while preserving adequate coverage. | - Introduce a “Weekly Pulse Check” survey sent via SurveyMonkey on Fridays, capturing crew morale scores; set a trigger in Power BI to alert the operations manager when the score falls below 70% so corrective actions can be initiated within 24 hours. | - Launch a gamified recognition program through Bonusly where team members earn points for meeting KPIs and completing micro‑learning modules, with quarterly bonus payouts tied to cumulative point totals to boost motivation and reduce turnover risk. | - Implement a structured “Shift Handoff Huddle” using Microsoft Teams Planner, assigning a 10‑minute slot at the start of every shift for cross‑training updates and brief wellness checks, tracked by attendance logs to ensure ≥95% compliance across all shifts.
rationale: All recommendations directly address crew overwork and demotivation with specific tools and measurable actions. Each is actionable with clear steps, uses concrete systems (Kronos, SurveyMonkey, Power BI, Bonusly, Teams Planner), and includes measurable targets. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to the need for integration of multiple tools (e.g., Kronos with labor analytics, Power BI alerts, Bonusly payouts), which requires cross-functional coordination and budget beyond existing tools—moderate effort, not trivial.
